metadata.title: Diagnosis Before Rewrite: See the Plan First
metadata.description_blurb: Asking for the finished artifact first is the most common prompting mistake. One habit fixes it: ask for a diagnosis and plan before the rewrite — you can correct a plan, but not a draft you cannot inspect.

beats.narration_text: She pasted the methods section and asked Claude to improve it. Claude rewrote it — fluently, completely, confidently. She read the rewrite and found things she liked and things that were wrong, and one she could not tell either way without going back to the original. The original was still in the attachment. But the rewrite was in front of her and it was easier to edit the rewrite than start over. She accepted an artifact she could not fully audit. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, improve the methods section

beats.narration_text: The artifact trap: a finished draft is harder to evaluate than a plan. A rewritten paragraph competes with the original for your attention. A diagnosis — this paragraph has two claims that contradict each other; severity high — is something you can agree or disagree with before any text changes. Correcting a plan costs seconds. Correcting a draft costs the effort of unpacking what was done before you can specify what you wanted instead.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact trap — draft competes with original / plan is inspectable before any text changes

beats.narration_text: The diagnosis prompt: 'Do not rewrite yet — diagnosis first. Read this section and identify issues: issue, severity, reason. List them as bullets.' That is the entire change to the workflow. One sentence added before the artifact request. Claude returns a diagnosis. You read it. You agree with some, correct others, identify one it missed. Now you have a plan you approved — and the rewrite executes that plan, not Claude's guess.
beats.new_visual_element: Diagnosis prompt structure — issue / severity / reason bullets, no rewrite yet

beats.narration_text: The diagnosis output is inspectable in a way the rewrite is not. You can check whether each flagged issue matches your concerns. You can disagree with a severity rating. You can add an issue Claude missed. You can reject the entire diagnosis and write your own. None of that is possible if Claude has already rewritten the section — at that point, you are editing an artifact you did not author, using criteria you have not stated.
beats.new_visual_element: Inspectable diagnosis — agree / correct severity / add missing issue / reject all

beats.narration_text: The most common prompting mistake is asking for the artifact before asking for the plan. Diagnosis before rewrite fixes this for revision tasks. Spec before code fixes it for implementation tasks. Plan before execution fixes it for agentic tasks. The pattern is the same in every register: the step before the artifact is the step that makes the artifact auditable. Skip it and you are editing Claude's guess, not verifying your spec.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — diagnosis → plan approved → rewrite executes the plan you approved

beats.narration_text: Add the diagnosis step to your next revision request. Paste this.

props.command: Do not rewrite yet. Read this section and produce a bullet-point diagnosis: for each issue, give (1) the issue name, (2) severity — high / medium / low, (3) the reason it is a problem. After I review and approve the diagnosis, I will ask for the rewrite. [paste section here]

beats.narration_text: If this stops you from editing a draft you did not approve, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
